title: Convex surfaces and grid diagrams
speaker: Ivan Dynnikov
abstract: Workshop on Flows, Foliations and Contact Structures 2015-2016 Monday, December 7, 2015 - 08:00 to Friday, December 11, 2015 - 12:00 This workshop is part of the topical program "Geometric Structures on 3-Manifolds" which will take place during the 2015-2016 academic year at the Institute for Advanced Study. This workshop will focus on the relations between flows, 2-dimensional foliations, and contact structures on 3-manifolds.
tags: Workshop on Flows Foliations and Contact Structures

proof_stages.label: Rectangular diagrams as Legendrian link encodings
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates replacing ordinary knot projections by rectangular/grid diagrams and explains how each such diagram on the torus gives two associated Legendrian links, one for each of the contact structures xi+ and xi-.

proof_stages.label: Elementary moves and independence of the two Legendrian links
proof_stages.summary: Isotopies, exchange moves, and type-one/type-two stabilizations are related to Legendrian isotopy and stabilization, leading to the theorem that a destabilization of one associated Legendrian link can be realized while preserving the other, with consequences such as decidability and braid/contact corollaries.

proof_stages.label: Rectangular diagrams of surfaces
proof_stages.summary: The knot-diagram formalism is extended from vertices to compatible rectangles, from which embedded surfaces in S^3, their square tilings, and their rectangular boundary diagrams are constructed.

proof_stages.label: Move calculus for rectangular surfaces
proof_stages.summary: A proposed complete calculus of surface diagrams is introduced using torus isotopy, bubble moves, wrinkle moves, exchange moves, and type-one/type-two flypes, with completeness stated at least for closed surfaces.

proof_stages.label: Convex-surface interpretation and dividing curves
proof_stages.summary: Rectangular surfaces are interpreted as convex surfaces for both contact structures, their dividing curves are read combinatorially from the tiling, boundary representability is constrained by relative Thurston-Bennequin framing, and the move calculus is sorted by which convex equivalence class it preserves.

proof_stages.label: Rigid diagrams, dividing slopes, and normal-surface computation
proof_stages.summary: Rigid rectangular diagrams are used to make dividing slopes on incompressible tori into computable isotopy invariants, yielding explicit Legendrian cable representatives and connecting the formalism to normal-surface algorithms.

proof_stages.label: Mirror diagrams and separated type-one/type-two calculus
proof_stages.summary: Rectangular surface data are compressed to mirror diagrams whose circuits encode boundary components, with moves that are complete up to adding or filling holes and that can be separated into type-one and type-two parts preserving the xi+ and xi- dividing data respectively.

proof_stages.label: Applications to Legendrian equivalence via surface tilings
proof_stages.summary: The speaker proposes using the surface and mirror-diagram calculus to test Legendrian equivalence: under additional hypotheses, equivalent rectangular boundaries should reproduce the same surface tilings after exchange moves, a strategy illustrated on several atlas and annulus examples.

examples.name: Simplest rectangular diagrams of a sphere and a torus
examples.note: The two-sphere and the Heegaard-torus examples make the surface encoded by a collection of compatible rectangles visually concrete, including how the induced tiling is read.
examples.engagement_reason: he uses the rectangular diagrams to identify the sphere/torus surfaces and reasons through the torus tiling via horizontal/vertical lines and four-valent vertices

examples.name: Trefoil Seifert surface and the boundary framing obstruction
examples.note: The trefoil Seifert-surface diagram illustrates why a prescribed rectangular boundary may need stabilization so that the relative Thurston-Bennequin framing condition is nonpositive.
examples.engagement_reason: he checks the trefoil Seifert-surface boundary obstruction by comparing the two associated Thurston--Bennequin framings and explaining the needed stabilization

passes.reason: the trefoil Seifert surface is a minimal-genus surface and is used to exhibit the positive boundary-framing obstruction to the naive simplest diagram.
The minimal-genus trefoil Seifert surface is used to exhibit the positive boundary-framing obstruction to the unstabilized diagram.
the trefoil surface is a minimal-genus Seifert surface and its maximal-framing boundary gives an obstruction to the desired rectangular presentation
the minimal-genus trefoil Seifert surface is used to exhibit a boundary-framing obstruction to the unstabilized diagram
the genus-one trefoil Seifert surface is genus-minimizing and its boundary framing blocks the naïve simplest-boundary construction until stabilization.
The trefoil Seifert surface is the minimal-genus genus-one surface, and its boundary framing is used as an obstruction forcing stabilization.
the trefoil Seifert surface is a minimal-genus surface and is used to exhibit the positive boundary-framing obstruction to the naive construction.
Used as a framing obstruction showing that the simplest trefoil boundary cannot realize the desired rectangular Seifert surface without stabilization; the Seifert surface is minimal-genus.
used to show the simplest trefoil boundary is obstructed by positive relative framing, while the Seifert surface is minimal-genus/threshold-framed
The trefoil boundary framing gives a +1 Thurston--Bennequin obstruction to the intended surface presentation, while the Seifert surface is genus-minimizing.
the trefoil Seifert surface exposes the boundary Thurston--Bennequin framing obstruction, with a tight minimal-genus/framing situation
The minimal-genus trefoil Seifert surface is used to show the simplest trefoil boundary framing violates the required nonpositive Thurston-Bennequin condition.

examples.name: The (1,2)-cable of the right-hand trefoil
examples.note: This cable is used as an application showing how rigid rectangular diagrams and dividing slopes on an incompressible torus give explicit representatives of Legendrian types that were previously classified abstractly.
examples.engagement_reason: he analyzes the cable diagrams via moves, rigidity, satellite tori, and the slopes/dividing curves distinguishing the two representatives

passes.reason: the example concerns the two non-destabilizable Legendrian representatives of a cable knot, i.e. peak representatives in the stabilization order.

as used here the displayed cable representatives are nondestabilizable Legendrian peaks in a standard infinite cable family.

It supplies nondestabilizable Legendrian representatives of the cable, top elements in the stabilization order, as a classification illustration.

examples.name: Zero-framing disk with a contact tangency
examples.note: The disk whose Legendrian boundary has zero relative framing is used to illustrate why the mirror-diagram formalism allows an annular collar and controlled tangencies rather than only honest Legendrian boundary.
examples.engagement_reason: he invokes the zero-framing disk with a single contact tangency as an allowable case of the formalism but does not carry out a concrete diagrammatic computation on it

passes.reason: a disk with zero relative framing is a limiting/maximal-framing case, realized only by allowing a collapsed/tangent boundary feature.
A disk with collapsed or tangent boundary data and zero relative framing is a limiting case showing the strict Legendrian-boundary disk construction fails.
the zero-framing disk uses a collapsed annular boundary/contact-tangency case at the boundary value of the framing condition
the zero-framing disk is a boundary/collapsed-annulus case used to show the naive Legendrian-boundary description fails
the zero-framing disk uses a collapsed annular side and a single contact tangency at the critical tb=0 boundary case.
A disk with collapsed/zero relative framing is a limiting base case, used to show the no-tangency Legendrian-boundary version cannot exist.
the zero-framing disk is a collapsed disk/annulus boundary case used to show the no-tangency Legendrian-boundary setup is too restrictive.
Illustrates the enlarged almost-Legendrian boundary formalism; the disk has a collapsed boundary side/contact tangency and lies at zero, the limiting relative framing.
illustrates a limiting allowed disk with collapsed annular side and one contact tangency realizing the zero-framing threshold
It illustrates the almost-Legendrian formalism as a zero-framing boundary case with a collapsed annular side and a single contact tangency.
the zero-framing disk is a limiting collapsed almost-Legendrian boundary case with one contact tangency
The zero-framing disk is a boundary case with a collapsed annular side/contact tangency, showing the ordinary Legendrian-boundary disk model would fail.

examples.name: Six-crossing Legendrian-atlas pair with a rigid diagram
examples.note: A pair whose Legendrian equivalence is listed as unknown is used to demonstrate the proposed tiling obstruction: a two-valent vertex in one Seifert-surface tiling would have to be eliminable by exchanges, but the other boundary diagram is rigid.
examples.engagement_reason: he applies the surface-tiling method to the six-crossing atlas pair, using rigidity and a two-valent vertex in a constructed Seifert surface to argue toward non-equivalence

passes.reason: the rigid six-crossing diagram is an isolated no-exchange-move case used as an obstruction to Legendrian equivalence of the atlas pair.
The rigid atlas diagram has no exchange moves, obstructing the counterpart tiling and serving as evidence against Legendrian equivalence.

the six-crossing pair uses a rigid no-exchange diagram as an obstruction to Legendrian equivalence
the rigid six-crossing atlas diagram lies on the boundary of the exchange-move graph and obstructs reproducing the two-valent tiling, aiming to prove non-equivalence.
The rigid six-crossing diagram is an isolated/no-exchange-move case used as an obstruction to the possible Legendrian equivalence of the atlas pair.
the six-crossing atlas pair is used as an obstruction to Legendrian equivalence, with rigidity meaning no exchange moves are available.
Deployed as an obstruction/candidate proof of non-equivalence in the Legendrian atlas; the rigid diagram admits no exchange moves, making it locally minimal.
used as an obstruction to Legendrian equivalence because a needed two-valent tiling cannot be reproduced on the rigid diagram
The pair is used to obstruct Legendrian equivalence, with rigidity meaning the relevant diagram admits no exchange moves.
the rigid six-crossing atlas pair is used to obstruct Legendrian equivalence by forbidding the needed exchange-move tiling
The rigid no-exchange six-crossing diagram is used to obstruct the proposed Legendrian equivalence, making it an irreducible extremal diagram.

examples.name: Rotated atlas pair with matching colored tilings
examples.note: The 180-degree-rotated pair tests the same strategy and shows that quite different rectangular surfaces can nevertheless realize the same tiling pattern, so this attempt does not disprove the conjectural criterion.
examples.engagement_reason: he constructs corresponding Seifert surfaces for the rotated pair and checks that the colored rectangles have the same vertex-sharing tiling pattern

passes.reason: the rotated atlas pair is presented as a positive matching-tiling test case rather than as a structurally exceptional or adversarial object.
The two atlas diagrams are related by a 180-degree rotation with matching colored tilings, supporting rather than refuting the proposed matching.

the half-turn-related atlas diagrams with matching colored tilings support rather than obstruct the conjectural equivalence.
The pair is related by a 180-degree rotation and has matching colored tilings, so it supports rather than contradicts the proposed behavior.

Used as a positive check rather than a disproof; the pair is related by a 180-degree rotation and has matching colored tiling combinatorics.

examples.name: Rigid knots cobounding a contact-tangent annulus
examples.note: This constructed pair, with vanishing known algebraic invariant and an annulus tangent to the contact structure along the boundary, motivates the need for the rectangular convex-surface technique to challenge a claimed equivalence theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: he constructs rigid diagrams cobounding a contact-tangent annulus and discusses computed invariants and rigidity as part of trying to distinguish the knots

passes.reason: the rigid cobounding-annulus pair is proposed as the simplest rigid obstruction and intended counterexample to the claim that such knots must be Legendrian equivalent.
